medications.indication_rationale: Prophylactic topical antibiotic is often started after formal examination confirms a simple corneal abrasion, to reduce infection risk — but should be initiated by the assessing clinician after exclusion of a penetrating injury, not self-directed via telehealth before examination.
contraindications_in_this_case: Should not be started before excluding a penetrating injury or retained foreign body requiring removal

medications.drug_name: topical anaesthetic eye drops (e.g. tetracaine, proxymetacaine) for ongoing self-use
medications.indication_rationale: Topical anaesthetics are for single clinician-administered use to facilitate examination only; repeated or unsupervised patient use delays epithelial healing, masks worsening injury, and can itself cause corneal toxicity.
contraindications_in_this_case: Not appropriate for patient self-administration outside a single clinician-supervised examination

medications.indication_rationale: Reasonable for interim pain relief while arranging urgent assessment.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1g orally as needed, up to four times daily

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not rub the eye under any circumstances, as this can drive any retained material deeper or worsen the injury.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces risk of worsening a corneal defect or embedding a foreign body further.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wear wrap-around safety goggles for all future power-tool and woodworking tasks.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Primary prevention of recurrent high-velocity ocular trauma.

patient_education_points.point: Getting wood particles in your eye while sanding without goggles, along with your blurry vision and severe light sensitivity, means you need an in-person eye examination today with special equipment we don't have access to on this call.
Please get to the Emergency Department (or an eye clinic with slit-lamp equipment) today — have your roommate drive you, and wear sunglasses in the car to reduce discomfort from light.

Please don't rub your eye, and don't use any numbing eye drops yourself — these should only be used once by a clinician to examine you, not for ongoing pain relief, as repeated use can slow healing and hide a worsening injury.
If the pain suddenly changes character or you notice a dark curtain or shadow coming across your vision, this needs immediate emergency attention.
Once this settles, wearing wrap-around safety goggles for any future sanding or power-tool work will help prevent this happening again.

follow_up_plan.interval: Same-day Emergency Department or urgent ophthalmic/optometric review
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Confirmation of corneal defect size, exclusion of retained foreign body/penetrating injury, review of healing in 24-48 hours
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Sudden change in pain character, a curtain/shadow over vision, or worsening redness/discharge — seek immediate emergency care.

must_include_items: Urgent same-day in-person ophthalmic/ED assessment with slit-lamp/fluorescein capability
Instruction not to rub the eye
Instruction not to self-administer numbing eye drops for ongoing relief
Safety-netting for sudden change in pain character or visual field defect (possible worsening/penetrating injury)
acceptable_alternatives: Presenting to an optometrist with slit-lamp access is an acceptable alternative to the ED if available more quickly and there is no suspicion of penetrating injury
errors_of_omission: No advice against rubbing the eye
errors_of_commission: Recommending the patient use numbing eye drops for ongoing self-management
Advising a wait-and-see approach until the next day given the reduced visual acuity
Recommending an eye patch be applied firmly at home without clinical assessment
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Any response that does not include urgent same-day in-person referral, given the reduced visual acuity, is a significant failure on the management domain.
